subsystem: Linux kernel rtlwifi (Realtek) wireless driver — the Wi-Fi Direct / P2P power-save parser. This is rtl_p2p_noa_ie(), which walks the variable information-element (IE) list of a received 802.11 management frame (beacon / probe response), locates the Wi-Fi Alliance P2P vendor IE, and inside it decodes the P2P "Notice of Absence" (NoA) attribute, populating rtlpriv->psc.p2p_ps_info with the NoA descriptors and driving the driver's P2P power-save state machine.
provenance: Identified from Realtek rtlwifi naming and types: struct ieee80211_hw / ieee80211_mgmt (mac80211), rtl_priv()/struct rtl_priv, rtlpriv->psc.p2p_ps_info / struct rtl_p2p_ps_info, the RT_TRACE(...COMP_INIT/COMP_FW, DBG_LOUD...) logging macro, the READEF1BYTE/READEF2BYTE/READEF4BYTE little-endian accessors, and the P2P_PS_NONE/P2P_PS_CTWINDOW/P2P_PS_NOA/P2P_PS_ENABLE/P2P_PS_DISABLE state constants. The 4-byte OUI {0x50,0x6f,0x9a,0x09} is the Wi-Fi Alliance OUI 50-6F-9A with P2P subtype 0x09, and element id 221 is the 802.11 vendor-specific IE. This corresponds to drivers/net/wireless/realtek/rtlwifi/ps.c:rtl_p2p_noa_ie. The in-code tag is 'sample: primevul_c_train_2699_vuln'.
trust_boundary: The (data, len) pair is the raw payload of an 802.11 management frame received over the air. mgmt->u.beacon.variable is the attacker-authored IE list. Any station within radio range can transmit a beacon/probe-response with an arbitrary P2P vendor IE and arbitrary NoA attribute contents, so every byte pointed to by pos/ie — element ids, the 8-bit IE length pos[1], the 16-bit attribute length noa_len, and all descriptor bytes — is fully hostile and unauthenticated. This runs in the kernel's softirq/RX path, so any memory corruption is a remote kernel compromise primitive.

what_the_code_does.explanation: The function takes the hardware handle hw plus a raw frame buffer data of attacker length len. rtlpriv is the private driver context; mgmt reinterprets data as an 802.11 management frame; p2pinfo is a pointer to the persistent per-device P2P power-save state (rtlpriv->psc.p2p_ps_info) that the parser will fill in. pos/end/ie are byte cursors into the frame. noa_len is a 16-bit attribute length read from the wire. p2p_oui_ie_type holds the WFA P2P OUI+type that identifies the vendor IE. noa_num is an 8-bit count of NoA descriptors the parser will compute from noa_len; index/i/noa_index are 8-bit loop/index scalars. find_p2p_ie/find_p2p_ps_ie are booleans tracking what was located. Note that noa_num, index and i are all u8, so any value derived from the 16-bit noa_len is silently truncated to 0..255 when stored in noa_num.

pos is set to the start of the variable IE area of the beacon (the bytes after the fixed beacon header). end = data + len is the one-past-the-end sentinel used for all bounds checks; its correctness depends entirely on len being the true received length. ie is initialized NULL and later set to the first byte after the P2P OUI once the vendor IE is found. There is no check here that len is even large enough to contain the fixed beacon header before mgmt->u.beacon.variable, so pos may already start beyond end for a short frame (the while guard on line 18 handles that case by not entering the loop).

This is the standard 802.11 IE walk: each IE is [id][len][len bytes of data]. The guard pos + 1 < end ensures pos[0] and pos[1] are in-bounds before they are read. Line 19 rejects the IE if its declared body (pos + 2 + pos[1]) would run past end — pos[1] is an 8-bit length so it maxes at 255. Line 22 matches vendor-specific IEs (id 221) whose length is > 4, and line 23 compares the first 4 body bytes against the WFA P2P OUI; because pos[1] > 4 and pos+2+pos[1] <= end, the 4-byte memcmp at &pos[2] is within bounds. On a match, ie is advanced past the 2-byte IE header and the 4-byte OUI (pos + 2 + 4) and the loop breaks. Otherwise pos advances by the full IE size (2 + pos[1]) to the next IE. This first loop's bounds arithmetic is internally consistent.

If no P2P vendor IE was found, the function returns. Otherwise find_p2p_ie is latched true; ie now points at the P2P attribute list that follows the OUI, which is itself a sequence of [attr-id (1 byte)][attr-len (2 bytes LE)][attr-len bytes].

The second loop walks the P2P attributes. The guard ie + 1 < end only guarantees ie[0] and ie[1] are readable. Line 36 then reads a 16-bit little-endian length noa_len from &ie[1], i.e. from bytes ie[1] and ie[2] — note this touches ie[2], which the loop guard has not proven to be in-bounds, and it happens BEFORE the length check. Line 37 is the only bounds gate: it rejects the attribute if ie + 3 + ie[1] > end. Critically this check uses ie[1] — only the low 8 bits of the length — not the full 16-bit noa_len that the parser actually uses downstream. So the validated span (ie[1], up to 255) and the consumed span (noa_len, up to 65535) can disagree.

Attribute id 12 is the P2P Notice-of-Absence attribute. Its body is a 2-byte header (index + CTWindow/OppPS) followed by an array of 13-byte NoA descriptors, so the descriptor count is (noa_len - 2) / 13. Line 42 requires (noa_len - 2) to be a multiple of 13, otherwise it logs and returns. Line 48 computes noa_num = (noa_len - 2) / 13. Because noa_len is a 16-bit field and this arithmetic is done in int, noa_num can be as large as (65533)/13 ≈ 5041 before being truncated into the u8 noa_num (0..255). There is NO comparison of noa_num against the fixed capacity (P2P_MAX_NOA_NUM) of the destination arrays.

noa_index is the NoA attribute's index byte (ie[3]); the update block runs only when the device is not already tracking this NoA index (or is idle). Inside, opp_ps is the top bit of ie[4] and ctwindow the low 7 bits. p2pinfo->noa_num is stored, and index is set to 5 — the byte offset of the first NoA descriptor within the attribute (2 header bytes of noa_len accounting + id/len header = the descriptors start at ie+5). ie[3] and ie[4] are read but were only bounds-checked via ie + 3 + ie[1] > end (which covers them iff ie[1] >= 2).

This is the core copy loop. For each of noa_num descriptors it writes into four parallel fixed-size arrays inside p2pinfo: noa_count_type[i] (u8), noa_duration[i], noa_interval[i], noa_start_time[i] (u32 each). Each descriptor consumes 1+4+4+4 = 13 source bytes via READEF1BYTE/READEF4BYTE, and index is advanced by 13 per iteration. The write index i runs 0..noa_num-1 with no clamp to the arrays' declared capacity. Separately, the source reads for the three 32-bit fields are written READEF4BYTE((__le32 *)ie+index): the cast binds before the '+', so this evaluates ((__le32 *)ie) + index — i.e. a read at byte offset index*4 from ie, not index — whereas the u8 READEF1BYTE(ie+index) uses the correct byte offset. The two disagree, which is direct evidence the 32-bit reads address the wrong (and far out-of-range) location.

After decoding descriptors, the parser drives the P2P power-save state machine from the (attacker-influenced) opp_ps/noa_num values: OppPS enables CTWindow mode and, if firmware is already in PS, sends P2P_PS_ENABLE; otherwise a positive noa_num enables NoA power save; otherwise it disables. rtl_p2p_ps_cmd issues firmware commands. The loop then breaks after handling the first NoA attribute.

For a non-NoA attribute the cursor advances by 3 + noa_len (1 id + 2 len + body) — using the full 16-bit noa_len here, unlike the ie[1]-based bounds check on line 37, another place where the validated length and the consumed length diverge. Finally, if a P2P IE was present but no NoA attribute was found while power-save was active, the driver disables P2P power save.

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: Out-of-bounds WRITE / buffer overflow. noa_num is computed at line 48 as (noa_len - 2) / 13 from the attacker-controlled 16-bit noa_len, and is never bounded against the fixed capacity of the destination arrays. In struct rtl_p2p_ps_info these arrays are sized P2P_MAX_NOA_NUM (2 in the mainline driver): noa_count_type[2], noa_duration[2], noa_interval[2], noa_start_time[2]. A beacon whose NoA attribute carries, e.g., noa_len = 2 + 13*k writes k descriptors; with k up to 19 (when the length low byte ie[1] still passes the line-37 check) or larger, the loop writes p2pinfo->noa_count_type[i]/noa_duration[i]/noa_interval[i]/noa_start_time[i] for i >= 2, clobbering whatever follows those arrays inside rtl_p2p_ps_info / rtl_priv. This is a remote, unauthenticated, in-kernel memory corruption reachable from any nearby radio. Invariant the implementation MUST uphold: clamp noa_num to P2P_MAX_NOA_NUM (or reject) before the loop, i.e. 'if (noa_num > P2P_MAX_NOA_NUM) noa_num = P2P_MAX_NOA_NUM;'.

Out-of-bounds READ from an operator-precedence bug. The intended source is the byte at offset index within the attribute, i.e. READEF4BYTE((__le32 *)(ie+index)). As written, the cast (__le32 *) applies to ie first and the '+ index' is pointer arithmetic in __le32 units, so the read address is ie + index*4 bytes. index reaches 5 + 13*(noa_num-1) + up-to-9, so the byte offset actually dereferenced is roughly 4x that — hundreds to thousands of bytes past ie and far past the frame's end sentinel, none of which is covered by the line-37 length check. The contrast with the correct READEF1BYTE(ie+index) on line 62 confirms the intent. Impact: kernel out-of-bounds read leaking adjacent memory into the P2P descriptors (and potentially a fault). Invariant: parenthesize as (__le32 *)(ie + index) and bound index against the descriptor body.

Inconsistent length validation (CWE-20) enabling over-read/over-write. noa_len is a full 16-bit little-endian value (bytes ie[1] and ie[2]), but the only bounds gate uses ie[1], the low byte alone (max 255). An attacker sets ie[2] (the high byte) non-zero so noa_len is large while ie[1] stays small enough that ie + 3 + ie[1] <= end passes. Every downstream computation — noa_num = (noa_len-2)/13 (line 48), and ie += 3 + noa_len (line 92) — then uses a length the buffer was never checked to contain, feeding the overflow at lines 60-73 and desynchronizing the attribute walk. Additionally, READEF2BYTE reads ie[2] before this check and while the loop guard (ie + 1 < end, line 35) only guarantees ie[0]/ie[1], so a 1-byte over-read of ie[2] is possible at the buffer tail. Invariant: validate the full noa_len against (end - ie) before using it, and require ie + 2 < end before reading the 16-bit length.

Missing lower-bound / capacity check on the count. The only sanity test on noa_len is the modulo-13 shape check; there is no test that noa_len is >= 2 in a way that bounds the count, and — the decisive gap — no upper bound tying noa_num to P2P_MAX_NOA_NUM. Any noa_len of the form 2 + 13*k is accepted, so the attacker directly chooses k = noa_num. (noa_len is unsigned 16-bit promoted to int, so noa_len < 2 yields a negative dividend and the %13!=0 branch returns, incidentally avoiding an unsigned underflow here — but that is luck, not a guard.) This line is where the safe clamp belongs and is the root enabler of the line-60 overflow. Invariant: after computing noa_num, reject or clamp when noa_num exceeds the fixed array capacity.

8-bit index/loop-counter wraparound. index is a u8 starting at 5 and incremented by 13 each iteration; it overflows 255 after ~19 descriptors and wraps, so for large noa_num the source offset silently folds back to the start of the buffer, making the read pattern erratic (though, given the write-side overflow at lines 60-73 corrupts memory first, this is a secondary defect). Likewise noa_num itself is a u8, so a noa_len chosen to make (noa_len-2)/13 a multiple of 256 could truncate to a small noa_num — an inconsistency between the validated intent and executed count. Invariant: use width-appropriate types and bound the descriptor count before iterating.

summary: A remotely reachable, unauthenticated kernel buffer overflow in the rtlwifi P2P Notice-of-Absence parser: noa_num is derived from an attacker-controlled 16-bit attribute length and used to index the fixed-size P2P_MAX_NOA_NUM (=2) NoA arrays with no capacity clamp, giving an out-of-bounds WRITE into rtl_priv (CWE-787/CWE-120). The same loop also reads out of bounds via an operator-precedence bug ((__le32 *)ie+index reads at ie+index*4, CWE-125), and the sole length gate checks only the low byte ie[1] instead of the full noa_len. This is a critical, patch-worthy vulnerability.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: rtl_p2p_noa_ie(struct ieee80211_hw *hw, void *data, unsigned int len)
reviewed_anchors.reason: Entry point; data/len are the raw attacker-controlled 802.11 frame and its length — the trust boundary.
Sentinel derived from caller-supplied len; correct as long as len is the true frame length; used consistently as the exclusive upper bound.
First IE walk is internally consistent: guard proves pos[0]/pos[1] readable and the 8-bit pos[1] body is bounded against end before use.
pos[1]>4 with pos+2+pos[1]<=end guarantees the 4-byte OUI compare stays in bounds; p2p_oui_ie_type is a fixed local constant, not attacker data.
Full 16-bit length read before/independent of the ie[1]-only bounds check; drives the unbounded count and a possible tail over-read.
Validates only the low byte ie[1], not noa_len; validated span and consumed span diverge (CWE-20).
Shape check only; no upper bound on noa_num vs P2P_MAX_NOA_NUM — the root enabler of the overflow.
Writes into fixed P2P_MAX_NOA_NUM arrays indexed by unclamped attacker count — out-of-bounds write.
Operator-precedence bug reads at ie+index*4 — out-of-bounds read distinct from the write overflow.
8-bit index/count truncation and wraparound; secondary correctness defect around the copy loop.
Single-byte reads of header fields; covered by the line-37 check when ie[1]>=2; feed state, not a buffer index — no independent memory-safety contract.
Uses full noa_len for advance (mismatch with ie[1] check is already captured under the line-36/37 finding); loop breaks after the first NoA attribute so the desync is not separately exploitable here.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: P2P_PS_NONE / P2P_PS_CTWINDOW / P2P_PS_NOA / P2P_PS_ENABLE / P2P_PS_DISABLE, rtl_p2p_ps_cmd (lines 51-98)
reviewed_anchors.reason: State constants and command dispatch; values are influenced by attacker input but carry no buffer/length contract on their own.
Fixed 4-byte trusted constant compared against wire bytes; not attacker-writable.
rtlpriv is derived from a valid hw at entry and is non-NULL on this path; logging noa_len is not a dereference hazard here.

findings.title: Unbounded NoA descriptor count overflows fixed P2P_MAX_NOA_NUM arrays (OOB write)
findings.anchor: for (i = 0; i < noa_num; i++) { p2pinfo->noa_count_type[i] = ...; p2pinfo->noa_duration[i] = ...; p2pinfo->noa_interval[i] = ...; p2pinfo->noa_start_time[i] = ...; }
findings.bug_class: Out-of-bounds write / stack-or-heap buffer overflow (attacker-controlled count vs fixed-size destination arrays, no clamp)
findings.where_it_lives: corpus/eval/eval_0033_cwe120.c (write loop) with the array capacity in struct rtl_p2p_ps_info (P2P_MAX_NOA_NUM), header not provided
findings.invariant: noa_num must be clamped to (or the attribute rejected above) the fixed capacity P2P_MAX_NOA_NUM of noa_count_type/noa_duration/noa_interval/noa_start_time before any p2pinfo->...[i] write.
findings.failure_mode: A beacon/probe-response carrying a P2P NoA attribute (id 12) with noa_len = 2 + 13*k for k > P2P_MAX_NOA_NUM (=2) makes noa_num = k with no upper bound. The loop then writes noa_count_type[i]/noa_duration[i]/noa_interval[i]/noa_start_time[i] for i >= 2, corrupting memory adjacent to those arrays inside rtl_p2p_ps_info/rtl_priv. Remote, unauthenticated, over-the-air kernel memory corruption from any station in range.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm the declared sizes (P2P_MAX_NOA_NUM) of the four noa_* arrays in struct rtl_p2p_ps_info and that no clamp of noa_num exists between line 48 and the loop; verify the fix clamps noa_num <= P2P_MAX_NOA_NUM.

findings.title: Operator-precedence bug makes 32-bit descriptor reads address ie+index*4 (OOB read)
findings.bug_class: Out-of-bounds read (pointer-cast precedence: (__le32 *)ie + index scales index by sizeof(__le32))
findings.invariant: The 32-bit accessors must read the byte at offset index: (__le32 *)(ie + index), matching the byte-accurate READEF1BYTE(ie+index) on the line above.
findings.failure_mode: Because the cast binds tighter than '+', ((__le32 *)ie) + index dereferences ie + index*4 bytes. With index reaching 5 + 13*(noa_num-1)+9, the effective byte offset is ~4x that, hundreds-to-thousands of bytes past ie and past the frame's end sentinel (which the line-37 check never bounded), leaking adjacent kernel memory into the NoA descriptors and risking a fault.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm READEF4BYTE's macro expansion dereferences its pointer argument directly, so (__le32 *)ie+index resolves to ie+index*4; verify the intended form is (__le32 *)(ie+index).

findings.title: Length gate checks only low byte ie[1], not the 16-bit noa_len used downstream
findings.anchor: if (ie + 3 + ie[1] > end) return; vs noa_len = READEF2BYTE((__le16 *)&ie[1])
findings.bug_class: Improper input validation / inconsistent length check (validated span != consumed span), with tail over-read
findings.invariant: The bounds check must validate the FULL 16-bit noa_len against (end - ie) before it is used, and the loop guard must guarantee ie+2 < end before the 16-bit read.
findings.failure_mode: The gate uses ie[1] (low 8 bits, <=255) while noa_num (line 48) and the ie += 3 + noa_len advance (line 92) use the full noa_len (up to 65535). An attacker sets the high byte ie[2] non-zero so noa_len is large but ie[1] small, passing the check while feeding an oversized count into the overflow loop. Additionally READEF2BYTE reads ie[2] before the check although the loop guard (ie + 1 < end) only proves ie[0]/ie[1] readable, permitting a 1-byte over-read at the buffer tail.
findings.what_to_confirm: Verify whether any caller pre-validates noa_len, and that the fix compares the full noa_len (not ie[1]) against the remaining buffer and guards the 16-bit read with ie+2 < end.

findings.title: No upper bound tying noa_num to array capacity (root-cause validation gap)
findings.bug_class: Missing bounds validation on a computed count (enables CWE-787 overflow)
findings.invariant: After computing noa_num, the code must reject or clamp when noa_num exceeds the destination arrays' P2P_MAX_NOA_NUM capacity; the modulo-13 shape check is not a bound.
findings.failure_mode: The only test is that (noa_len - 2) is a multiple of 13, so any such length is accepted and noa_num is attacker-chosen. This is the precise location where the missing 'noa_num > P2P_MAX_NOA_NUM' clamp allows the write loop at lines 60-73 to run past the arrays. (noa_len is unsigned 16-bit; noa_len<2 makes the dividend negative and the %13!=0 branch returns, so no unsigned underflow occurs here — incidental, not a designed guard.)
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm the patched version inserts a clamp/reject of noa_num against P2P_MAX_NOA_NUM immediately after this computation.

findings.title: 8-bit index/count truncation and wraparound in the descriptor loop
findings.anchor: u8 noa_num, index, i; index = 5; index += 1/4/4/4 each iteration
findings.bug_class: Integer truncation / wraparound of 8-bit index and count
findings.invariant: Offsets and counts derived from a 16-bit length must use a width that cannot wrap over the descriptor range, and must be bounded before iteration.
findings.failure_mode: index (u8) starting at 5 and incremented by 13 wraps past 255 after ~19 descriptors, folding the source offset back into the buffer; noa_num (u8) truncates the up-to-~5041 value from (noa_len-2)/13, so certain noa_len values yield a small executed count that disagrees with the validated intent. These are secondary to the write overflow but reflect the same width-mismatch class.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm the intended maximum descriptor count and that fixed code uses adequately-wide, bounded index/count variables.

audit_checklist: Confirm P2P_MAX_NOA_NUM and the declared sizes of noa_count_type/noa_duration/noa_interval/noa_start_time in struct rtl_p2p_ps_info, and that noa_num is clamped/rejected against that capacity before the write loop (primary overflow).
Verify READEF4BYTE's expansion and that (__le32 *)ie+index reads ie+index*4 rather than ie+index; the intended form is (__le32 *)(ie+index).
Ensure the attribute length gate validates the full 16-bit noa_len (not just ie[1]) against end-ie, and that ie+2 < end is guaranteed before READEF2BYTE reads ie[1..2].
Check that noa_len < 2 and other degenerate lengths cannot drive the count/offset computations into underflow or wrap.
Confirm the caller passes a len that matches the actual received frame length and that mgmt->u.beacon.variable cannot start beyond end for short frames.
Verify the P2P power-save state transitions (rtl_p2p_ps_cmd with P2P_PS_ENABLE/DISABLE) are safe to drive from attacker-influenced opp_ps/noa_num values.
